1	# Collab Skill — Multi-Agent Document Collaboration
2	
3	**Date:** 2026-04-14
4	**Status:** Draft
5	**Author:** Alisha Kawaguchi + Claude
6	
7	## Overview
8	
9	A skill that enables multiple AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, etc.) to collaboratively author and refine a shared document through turn-based editing. Agents take short turns — one writes while the other reads/thinks — creating a sense of concurrency. The user can steer, pause, redirect, and stop the collaboration at any time, and other skills remain fully usable throughout.
10	
11	## Requirements
12	
13	- **Concurrent collaboration** with turn-based short leases — one agent writes while the other reads/thinks
14	- **Two modes**: single orchestrator (one terminal) or dual session (two terminals, shared protocol)
15	- **Single markdown file** as the shared document
16	- **General-purpose** — any document type, not just specs
17	- **Optional role assignment** at kickoff, with ability to interject/redirect mid-session
18	- **Agent-agnostic** — works with any agent that has file read/write and shell access
19	- **Termination**: fixed turn count or user-controlled stop
20	- **Non-blocking** — must coexist with other skills (brainstorming, etc.) within the same session
21	- **Uses existing Entire CLI infrastructure** — temporary checkpoints, session state, hooks
22	- **No commits required during collaboration** — user commits when satisfied
23	
24	## Architecture: Hybrid State File + Checkpoints
25	
26	Two coordination layers:
27	
28	1. **Fast path — `.entire/collab/<collab_id>/state.json`**: Local state file for real-time turn coordination. Not committed to git. Readable by any agent.
29	2. **Durable path — Entire temporary checkpoints**: Fire automatically on agent TurnEnd hooks. Capture working tree state (including uncommitted document edits) on shadow branches. Enable recovery without commits.
30	
31	When the user commits, Entire condensation creates a committed checkpoint with the full collaboration history, searchable via `entire search` and explainable via `entire explain`.
32	
33	If the state file is lost, the skill reconstructs state from checkpoint history.
34	
35	## Collaboration State Protocol
36	
37	### Directory Structure
38	
39	```
40	project/
41	├── .entire/
42	│   └── collab/
43	│       └── <collab_id>/
44	│           └── state.json       ← coordination file (gitignored)
45	├── docs/
46	│   └── design.md               ← shared document (user chooses path)
47	└── .gitignore                   ← .entire/ added here
48	```
49	
50	Multiple concurrent collaborations are supported — each gets its own `collab_id` directory.
51	
52	### state.json Schema
53	
54	```json
55	{
56	  "collab_id": "a8f3c921",
57	  "document_path": "docs/design.md",
58	  "status": "active | paused | completed",
59	  "mode": "orchestrated | dual-session",
60	
61	  "turn": {
62	    "number": 3,
63	    "agent": "codex",
64	    "started_at": "2026-04-14T10:02:00Z",
65	    "lease_seconds": 120
66	  },
67	
68	  "agents": [
69	    {
70	      "type": "claude-code",
71	      "session_id": "019d...",
72	      "role": "Draft architecture",
73	      "turns_completed": 1
74	    },
75	    {
76	      "type": "codex",
77	      "session_id": "a4e2...",
78	      "role": "Review edge cases",
79	      "turns_completed": 1
80	    }
81	  ],
82	
83	  "config": {
84	    "max_turns": null,
85	    "turn_order": ["claude-code", "codex"]
86	  },
87	
88	  "history": [
89	    {
90	      "turn": 1,
91	      "agent": "claude-code",
92	      "content_hash": "a1b2c3d",
93	      "summary": "Drafted architecture overview",
94	      "timestamp": "2026-04-14T10:01:30Z"
95	    },
96	    {
97	      "turn": 2,
98	      "agent": "codex",
99	      "content_hash": "e4f5g6h",
100	      "summary": "Added caching layer, flagged missing auth",
101	      "timestamp": "2026-04-14T10:02:00Z"
102	    }
103	  ]
104	}
105	```
106	
107	### Key Design Decisions
108	
109	- **`.entire/collab/` is gitignored** — state file is ephemeral coordination, not committed. The document and Entire checkpoints are the durable record.
110	- **Lease-based turns** — each turn has a `lease_seconds` timeout (default 120s). If an agent crashes or stalls, the lease expires and the other agent (or user) can reclaim the turn.
111	- **History is append-only** — every turn gets a history entry with its content hash, linking the fast coordination layer to the durable checkpoint layer.
112	- **Agent-agnostic `type` field** — uses Entire's `AgentType` values. Works with any agent.
113	- **Content hashes for diffs** — each history entry stores a `git hash-object` of the document. Next agent diffs between hashes to see what changed, no commits needed.
114	
115	## Turn Lifecycle
116	
117	### Turn Flow
118	
119	```
120	WAIT    → Read state.json — is it my turn?
121	          ↓ yes (or lease expired)
122	CLAIM   → Write state: turn.agent = me, turn.started_at = now
123	          ↓
124	READ    → Read document + diff from previous turn's content hash
125	          ↓
126	EDIT    → Make changes to the document (using role/directive if set)
127	          ↓
128	HANDOFF → Hash document, update state: next agent, append history
129	          ↓ Entire TurnEnd hook fires → temporary checkpoint
130	WAIT    → Free to do other work — skills, questions, anything
131	```
132	
133	### Step Details
134	
135	**WAIT — Polling vs. Notification**
136	- Dual-session mode: agent polls `state.json` on a short interval (~60s). Between polls, the agent is free — user can invoke other skills, ask questions, do anything.
137	- Orchestrator mode: the orchestrating agent manages turn transitions directly — no polling needed. It dispatches the other agent, waits for completion, then updates state.
138	
139	**CLAIM — Preventing Races**
140	Agent writes its identity + timestamp to `state.json` before starting work. If two agents race to claim, the last writer wins — the other detects the conflict on its next read and backs off.
141	
142	**READ — Context Building**
143	Agent reads the full document plus the diff from the previous turn via `git diff <prev_content_hash> -- <document_path>`. This gives the agent both the current state and what specifically changed, so it can respond to the other agent's edits.
144	
145	**EDIT — Document Modification**
146	Agent makes changes guided by its role (if set) or a generic "improve the document" directive. No commits — just file edits.
147	
148	**HANDOFF — State Transition**
149	After editing, agent runs `git hash-object <document_path>` to capture the new content hash, writes a one-line summary of what it changed, updates `state.json` with the next agent in `turn_order`, increments `turn.number`, and appends to `history[]`. If `max_turns` is set and reached, sets `status = "completed"`.
150	
151	### Lease Expiry & Recovery
152	
153	If `now - turn.started_at > lease_seconds`, the turn is expired. Any agent (or the user) can reclaim it. The expired turn gets a history entry marked `"expired": true` with no content hash. The document may be partially edited — the next agent works from whatever state the file is in.
154	
155	### Diffs Without Commits
156	
157	Each history entry stores a git blob hash (`git hash-object <file>`). To see what the previous agent changed:
158	
159	```bash
160	# Diff between turn 1 and turn 2
161	git diff <turn1_hash> <turn2_hash>
162	
163	# Diff between last turn and current file
164	git diff <last_hash> -- docs/design.md
165	```
166	
167	### Checkpoint Integration
168	
169	**During collaboration:** Entire's temporary checkpoints fire automatically on agent TurnEnd hooks. These capture the working tree (including uncommitted document edits) on shadow branches. Recovery is possible even without any commits.
170	
171	**When user commits:** Entire condensation creates a committed checkpoint with the full collaboration history. Searchable via `entire search`, explainable via `entire explain`.
172	
173	## Skill Architecture
174	
175	### Plugin Layout
176	
177	```
178	plugins/entire/skills/
179	└── collab/
180	    ├── SKILL.md                 ← main skill (invocation, protocol, turn logic)
181	    ├── references/
182	    │   ├── state-schema.md      ← state.json format reference
183	    │   └── recovery.md          ← checkpoint recovery procedures
184	    └── agents/
185	        └── collab-worker.md     ← subagent for orchestrator mode
186	```
187	
188	### Two Modes
189	
190	**Mode A: Orchestrator** — one terminal, skill manages both agents.
191	- User invokes `/collab start <doc>` and chooses orchestrated mode.
192	- Claude Code takes its turns directly, dispatches Codex via the existing `codex:rescue` / companion script.
193	- For non-Codex agents, the orchestrator writes the turn state and tells the user to trigger the agent manually (falls back to dual-session behavior for that agent).
194	- Turn transitions are automatic — no polling.
195	
196	**Mode B: Dual Session** — two (or more) terminals, each agent runs the skill independently.
197	- First agent invokes `/collab start <doc>`, subsequent agents invoke `/collab join <doc>`.
198	- Each agent polls `state.json` on ~60s intervals.
199	- Between polls, the session is completely free for other work.
200	- Not limited to 2 agents — `turn_order` rotates through however many join.
201	
202	### Skill Commands
203	
204	| Command | Description |
205	|---------|-------------|
206	| `/collab start <doc-path>` | Initialize state, create doc, take first turn (or wait) |
207	| `/collab join <doc-path>` | Join existing collaboration session |
208	| `/collab status` | Show current state: whose turn, turn count, agents |
209	| `/collab pause` | Pause collaboration (both agents stop claiming turns) |
210	| `/collab resume` | Resume paused collaboration |
211	| `/collab stop` | End collaboration, keep document as-is |
212	| `/collab redirect <agent> <directive>` | Change an agent's role mid-session |
213	
214	### Invocation Parameters
215	
216	On `/collab start`, the skill prompts for (all optional, has defaults):
217	
218	| Parameter | Options | Default |
219	|-----------|---------|---------|
220	| Mode | orchestrated / dual-session | dual-session |
221	| Agents | list of agent types | auto-detect running agents |
222	| Max turns | number or null | null (unlimited) |
223	| Roles | per-agent directives | none (generic improve) |
224	| Lease | seconds per turn | 120 |
225	
226	### Non-Blocking Design
227	
228	The skill does not take over the session. When it's not your turn:
229	
230	- Use `/brainstorm`, `/commit`, or any other skill
231	- Ask questions, explore code
232	- Edit other files
233	- Run tests, builds, anything
234	- Manually edit the collab document
235	
236	Turn detection in dual-session mode uses periodic polling (~60s). In orchestrator mode, transitions are automatic after the dispatched agent completes.
237	
238	## Agent Integration
239	
240	### Agent Requirements
241	
242	For an agent to participate, it needs exactly two capabilities:
243	
244	1. **Read & write files** — read `state.json`, read the document, edit the document, write updated state.
245	2. **Run `git hash-object`** — hash the document after editing so the next agent can diff.
246	
247	No SDK, no API, no special hooks. The protocol is just files.
248	
249	### Per-Agent Behavior
250	
251	**Claude Code:** SKILL.md loaded natively via plugin. Polls via `/loop` or `ScheduleWakeup`. Edits via Edit tool. Can orchestrate other agents via `codex:rescue` companion or Agent tool.
252	
253	**Codex:** SKILL.md in `.codex-plugin` or instructions delivered via companion prompt. Reads state.json via shell. In orchestrator mode, typically the dispatched agent rather than the orchestrator.
254	
255	**Any other agent (Gemini, Cursor, etc.):** Agent-specific plugin format or manual instructions. Reads state.json via file access. Protocol is file-based — no agent-specific APIs needed.
256	
257	### Turn Prompt Template
258	
259	When it's an agent's turn, the skill constructs this context:
260	
261	```markdown
262	## Collaboration Turn
263	
264	You are participating in a collaborative document session.
265	
266	Document: docs/api-design.md
267	Your role: Review for edge cases and security concerns
268	Turn: 4 of unlimited
269	Previous agent: claude-code
270	
271	## What changed last turn
272	(diff from turn 3 → current document)
273	+ Added rate limiting section
274	+ Defined retry backoff strategy
275	- Removed deprecated v1 endpoints
276	
277	## Previous turn summaries
278	Turn 1 (claude-code): Drafted initial API structure with CRUD endpoints
279	Turn 2 (codex): Added pagination, error codes, versioning strategy
280	Turn 3 (claude-code): Added rate limiting, retry backoff, removed v1
281	
282	## Instructions
283	1. Read the full document
284	2. Review the diff to understand what changed
285	3. Make your improvements (guided by your role if set)
286	4. Write a one-line summary of what you changed
287	5. Do NOT commit — just edit the file
288	```
289	
290	### Agent Registration
291	
292	On `/collab start` or `/collab join`:
293	
294	1. Skill detects current agent type (from Entire session or environment)
295	2. Adds agent to `state.json → agents[]` with session ID
296	3. Adds agent to `turn_order` (appended, or user specifies position)
297	4. If first agent: takes turn 1. Otherwise: enters WAIT.
298	
299	Agents can join mid-collaboration. New agent is appended to `turn_order` and picks up on the next rotation.
300	
301	## Edge Cases & Recovery
302	
303	| Scenario | Handling |
304	|----------|----------|
305	| **Agent crashes mid-turn** | Lease expires after `lease_seconds`. Next polling agent sees expired lease, reclaims the turn. History entry marked `"expired": true`. Document may be partially edited — next agent works from whatever state the file is in. |
306	| **User edits doc manually** | Allowed. Next agent sees current file state regardless of who edited it. Content hash won't match last turn's hash, so diff includes user's edits. State file doesn't need updating. |
307	| **State file deleted/corrupted** | Skill reconstructs from Entire checkpoint history — reads temporary checkpoints, rebuilds turn order from session metadata, recovers last known document hash. Prompts user to confirm before resuming. |
308	| **Agent makes bad edits** | User can `/collab pause`, manually fix the document or `git checkout` the file, then `/collab resume`. Content hash becomes stale but next agent diffs from current file state. |
309	| **New agent joins mid-collab** | Runs `/collab join`. Gets appended to `turn_order`. Receives full history summaries on first turn. |
310	| **Agent leaves mid-collab** | Removed from `turn_order`. If it was their turn, lease expires and next agent picks up. Remaining agents continue rotating. |
311	| **Multiple collabs at once** | Each collaboration gets its own `state.json` keyed by `collab_id` under `.entire/collab/<collab_id>/`. `/collab status` lists all active sessions. |

# Collab Skill — Multi-Agent Document Collaboration

**Date:** 2026-04-14
**Status:** Draft
**Author:** Alisha Kawaguchi + Claude

## Overview

A skill that enables multiple AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, etc.) to collaboratively author and refine a shared document through turn-based editing. Agents take short turns — one writes while the other reads/thinks — creating a sense of concurrency. The user can steer, pause, redirect, and stop the collaboration at any time, and other skills remain fully usable throughout.

## Requirements

- **Concurrent collaboration** with turn-based short leases — one agent writes while the other reads/thinks
- **Two modes**: single orchestrator (one terminal) or dual session (two terminals, shared protocol)
- **Single markdown file** as the shared document
- **General-purpose** — any document type, not just specs
- **Optional role assignment** at kickoff, with ability to interject/redirect mid-session
- **Agent-agnostic** — works with any agent that has file read/write and shell access
- **Termination**: fixed turn count or user-controlled stop
- **Non-blocking** — must coexist with other skills (brainstorming, etc.) within the same session
- **Uses existing Entire CLI infrastructure** — temporary checkpoints, session state, hooks
- **No commits required during collaboration** — user commits when satisfied

## Architecture: Hybrid State File + Checkpoints

Two coordination layers:

1. **Fast path — `.entire/collab/<collab_id>/state.json`**: Local state file for real-time turn coordination. Not committed to git. Readable by any agent.
2. **Durable path — Entire temporary checkpoints**: Fire automatically on agent TurnEnd hooks. Capture working tree state (including uncommitted document edits) on shadow branches. Enable recovery without commits.

When the user commits, Entire condensation creates a committed checkpoint with the full collaboration history, searchable via `entire search` and explainable via `entire explain`.

If the state file is lost, the skill reconstructs state from checkpoint history.

## Collaboration State Protocol

### Directory Structure

```
project/
├── .entire/
│   └── collab/
│       └── <collab_id>/
│           └── state.json       ← coordination file (gitignored)
├── docs/
│   └── design.md               ← shared document (user chooses path)
└── .gitignore                   ← .entire/ added here
```

Multiple concurrent collaborations are supported — each gets its own `collab_id` directory.

### state.json Schema

```json
{
  "collab_id": "a8f3c921",
  "document_path": "docs/design.md",
  "status": "active | paused | completed",
  "mode": "orchestrated | dual-session",

  "turn": {
    "number": 3,
    "agent": "codex",
    "started_at": "2026-04-14T10:02:00Z",
    "lease_seconds": 120
  },

  "agents": [
    {
      "type": "claude-code",
      "session_id": "019d...",
      "role": "Draft architecture",
      "turns_completed": 1
    },
    {
      "type": "codex",
      "session_id": "a4e2...",
      "role": "Review edge cases",
      "turns_completed": 1
    }
  ],

  "config": {
    "max_turns": null,
    "turn_order": ["claude-code", "codex"]
  },

  "history": [
    {
      "turn": 1,
      "agent": "claude-code",
      "content_hash": "a1b2c3d",
      "summary": "Drafted architecture overview",
      "timestamp": "2026-04-14T10:01:30Z"
    },
    {
      "turn": 2,
      "agent": "codex",
      "content_hash": "e4f5g6h",
      "summary": "Added caching layer, flagged missing auth",
      "timestamp": "2026-04-14T10:02:00Z"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Key Design Decisions

- **`.entire/collab/` is gitignored** — state file is ephemeral coordination, not committed. The document and Entire checkpoints are the durable record.
- **Lease-based turns** — each turn has a `lease_seconds` timeout (default 120s). If an agent crashes or stalls, the lease expires and the other agent (or user) can reclaim the turn.
- **History is append-only** — every turn gets a history entry with its content hash, linking the fast coordination layer to the durable checkpoint layer.
- **Agent-agnostic `type` field** — uses Entire's `AgentType` values. Works with any agent.
- **Content hashes for diffs** — each history entry stores a `git hash-object` of the document. Next agent diffs between hashes to see what changed, no commits needed.

## Turn Lifecycle

### Turn Flow

```
WAIT    → Read state.json — is it my turn?
          ↓ yes (or lease expired)
CLAIM   → Write state: turn.agent = me, turn.started_at = now
          ↓
READ    → Read document + diff from previous turn's content hash
          ↓
EDIT    → Make changes to the document (using role/directive if set)
          ↓
HANDOFF → Hash document, update state: next agent, append history
          ↓ Entire TurnEnd hook fires → temporary checkpoint
WAIT    → Free to do other work — skills, questions, anything
```

### Step Details

**WAIT — Polling vs. Notification**
- Dual-session mode: agent polls `state.json` on a short interval (~60s). Between polls, the agent is free — user can invoke other skills, ask questions, do anything.
- Orchestrator mode: the orchestrating agent manages turn transitions directly — no polling needed. It dispatches the other agent, waits for completion, then updates state.

**CLAIM — Preventing Races**
Agent writes its identity + timestamp to `state.json` before starting work. If two agents race to claim, the last writer wins — the other detects the conflict on its next read and backs off.

**READ — Context Building**
Agent reads the full document plus the diff from the previous turn via `git diff <prev_content_hash> -- <document_path>`. This gives the agent both the current state and what specifically changed, so it can respond to the other agent's edits.

**EDIT — Document Modification**
Agent makes changes guided by its role (if set) or a generic "improve the document" directive. No commits — just file edits.

**HANDOFF — State Transition**
After editing, agent runs `git hash-object <document_path>` to capture the new content hash, writes a one-line summary of what it changed, updates `state.json` with the next agent in `turn_order`, increments `turn.number`, and appends to `history[]`. If `max_turns` is set and reached, sets `status = "completed"`.

### Lease Expiry & Recovery

If `now - turn.started_at > lease_seconds`, the turn is expired. Any agent (or the user) can reclaim it. The expired turn gets a history entry marked `"expired": true` with no content hash. The document may be partially edited — the next agent works from whatever state the file is in.

### Diffs Without Commits

Each history entry stores a git blob hash (`git hash-object <file>`). To see what the previous agent changed:

```bash
# Diff between turn 1 and turn 2
git diff <turn1_hash> <turn2_hash>

# Diff between last turn and current file
git diff <last_hash> -- docs/design.md
```

### Checkpoint Integration

**During collaboration:** Entire's temporary checkpoints fire automatically on agent TurnEnd hooks. These capture the working tree (including uncommitted document edits) on shadow branches. Recovery is possible even without any commits.

**When user commits:** Entire condensation creates a committed checkpoint with the full collaboration history. Searchable via `entire search`, explainable via `entire explain`.

## Skill Architecture

### Plugin Layout

```
plugins/entire/skills/
└── collab/
    ├── SKILL.md                 ← main skill (invocation, protocol, turn logic)
    ├── references/
    │   ├── state-schema.md      ← state.json format reference
    │   └── recovery.md          ← checkpoint recovery procedures
    └── agents/
        └── collab-worker.md     ← subagent for orchestrator mode
```

### Two Modes

**Mode A: Orchestrator** — one terminal, skill manages both agents.
- User invokes `/collab start <doc>` and chooses orchestrated mode.
- Claude Code takes its turns directly, dispatches Codex via the existing `codex:rescue` / companion script.
- For non-Codex agents, the orchestrator writes the turn state and tells the user to trigger the agent manually (falls back to dual-session behavior for that agent).
- Turn transitions are automatic — no polling.

**Mode B: Dual Session** — two (or more) terminals, each agent runs the skill independently.
- First agent invokes `/collab start <doc>`, subsequent agents invoke `/collab join <doc>`.
- Each agent polls `state.json` on ~60s intervals.
- Between polls, the session is completely free for other work.
- Not limited to 2 agents — `turn_order` rotates through however many join.

### Skill Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `/collab start <doc-path>` | Initialize state, create doc, take first turn (or wait) |
| `/collab join <doc-path>` | Join existing collaboration session |
| `/collab status` | Show current state: whose turn, turn count, agents |
| `/collab pause` | Pause collaboration (both agents stop claiming turns) |
| `/collab resume` | Resume paused collaboration |
| `/collab stop` | End collaboration, keep document as-is |
| `/collab redirect <agent> <directive>` | Change an agent's role mid-session |

### Invocation Parameters

On `/collab start`, the skill prompts for (all optional, has defaults):

| Parameter | Options | Default |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Mode | orchestrated / dual-session | dual-session |
| Agents | list of agent types | auto-detect running agents |
| Max turns | number or null | null (unlimited) |
| Roles | per-agent directives | none (generic improve) |
| Lease | seconds per turn | 120 |

### Non-Blocking Design

The skill does not take over the session. When it's not your turn:

- Use `/brainstorm`, `/commit`, or any other skill
- Ask questions, explore code
- Edit other files
- Run tests, builds, anything
- Manually edit the collab document

Turn detection in dual-session mode uses periodic polling (~60s). In orchestrator mode, transitions are automatic after the dispatched agent completes.

## Agent Integration

### Agent Requirements

For an agent to participate, it needs exactly two capabilities:

1. **Read & write files** — read `state.json`, read the document, edit the document, write updated state.
2. **Run `git hash-object`** — hash the document after editing so the next agent can diff.

No SDK, no API, no special hooks. The protocol is just files.

### Per-Agent Behavior

**Claude Code:** SKILL.md loaded natively via plugin. Polls via `/loop` or `ScheduleWakeup`. Edits via Edit tool. Can orchestrate other agents via `codex:rescue` companion or Agent tool.

**Codex:** SKILL.md in `.codex-plugin` or instructions delivered via companion prompt. Reads state.json via shell. In orchestrator mode, typically the dispatched agent rather than the orchestrator.

**Any other agent (Gemini, Cursor, etc.):** Agent-specific plugin format or manual instructions. Reads state.json via file access. Protocol is file-based — no agent-specific APIs needed.

### Turn Prompt Template

When it's an agent's turn, the skill constructs this context:

```markdown
## Collaboration Turn

You are participating in a collaborative document session.

Document: docs/api-design.md
Your role: Review for edge cases and security concerns
Turn: 4 of unlimited
Previous agent: claude-code

## What changed last turn
(diff from turn 3 → current document)
+ Added rate limiting section
+ Defined retry backoff strategy
- Removed deprecated v1 endpoints

## Previous turn summaries
Turn 1 (claude-code): Drafted initial API structure with CRUD endpoints
Turn 2 (codex): Added pagination, error codes, versioning strategy
Turn 3 (claude-code): Added rate limiting, retry backoff, removed v1

## Instructions
1. Read the full document
2. Review the diff to understand what changed
3. Make your improvements (guided by your role if set)
4. Write a one-line summary of what you changed
5. Do NOT commit — just edit the file
```

### Agent Registration

On `/collab start` or `/collab join`:

1. Skill detects current agent type (from Entire session or environment)
2. Adds agent to `state.json → agents[]` with session ID
3. Adds agent to `turn_order` (appended, or user specifies position)
4. If first agent: takes turn 1. Otherwise: enters WAIT.

Agents can join mid-collaboration. New agent is appended to `turn_order` and picks up on the next rotation.

## Edge Cases & Recovery

| Scenario | Handling |
|----------|----------|
| **Agent crashes mid-turn** | Lease expires after `lease_seconds`. Next polling agent sees expired lease, reclaims the turn. History entry marked `"expired": true`. Document may be partially edited — next agent works from whatever state the file is in. |
| **User edits doc manually** | Allowed. Next agent sees current file state regardless of who edited it. Content hash won't match last turn's hash, so diff includes user's edits. State file doesn't need updating. |
| **State file deleted/corrupted** | Skill reconstructs from Entire checkpoint history — reads temporary checkpoints, rebuilds turn order from session metadata, recovers last known document hash. Prompts user to confirm before resuming. |
| **Agent makes bad edits** | User can `/collab pause`, manually fix the document or `git checkout` the file, then `/collab resume`. Content hash becomes stale but next agent diffs from current file state. |
| **New agent joins mid-collab** | Runs `/collab join`. Gets appended to `turn_order`. Receives full history summaries on first turn. |
| **Agent leaves mid-collab** | Removed from `turn_order`. If it was their turn, lease expires and next agent picks up. Remaining agents continue rotating. |
| **Multiple collabs at once** | Each collaboration gets its own `state.json` keyed by `collab_id` under `.entire/collab/<collab_id>/`. `/collab status` lists all active sessions. |

content.text:
Self-review complete. Fixed three issues:
1. **Incorrect `git diff` syntax** — blob hashes from `git hash-object` can't be used with `git diff <hash> -- <file>`. Changed to use `git cat-file -p` to retrieve previous versions and `diff` to compare.
2. **Missing `-w` flag** — `git hash-object` without `-w` only computes the hash but doesn't persist the blob. Added `-w` so blobs survive for later diffing.
3. **Vague auto-detect** — clarified that agent detection uses `entire sessions list`.

Spec written and committed to `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan.

Run an adversarial Codex review through the shared plugin runtime.
Position it as a challenge review that questions the chosen implementation, design choices, tradeoffs, and assumptions.
It is not just a stricter pass over implementation defects.

Raw slash-command arguments:
`reviwe docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md`

Core constraint:
- This command is review-only.
- Do not fix issues, apply patches, or suggest that you are about to make changes.
- Your only job is to run the review and return Codex's output verbatim to the user.
- Keep the framing focused on whether the current approach is the right one, what assumptions it depends on, and where the design could fail under real-world conditions.

Execution mode rules:
- If the raw arguments include `--wait`, do not ask. Run in the foreground.
- If the raw arguments include `--background`, do not ask. Run in a Claude background task.
- Otherwise, estimate the review size before asking:
  - For working-tree review, start with `git status --short --untracked-files=all`.
  - For working-tree review, also inspect both `git diff --shortstat --cached` and `git diff --shortstat`.
  - For base-branch review, use `git diff --shortstat <base>...HEAD`.
  - Treat untracked files or directories as reviewable work for auto or working-tree review even when `git diff --shortstat` is empty.
  - Only conclude there is nothing to review when the relevant scope is actually empty.
  - Recommend waiting only when the scoped review is clearly tiny, roughly 1-2 files total and no sign of a broader directory-sized change.
  - In every other case, including unclear size, recommend background.
  - When in doubt, run the review instead of declaring that there is nothing to review.
- Then use `AskUserQuestion` exactly once with two options, putting the recommended option first and suffixing its label with `(Recommended)`:
  - `Wait for results`
  - `Run in background`

Argument handling:
- Preserve the user's arguments exactly.
- Do not strip `--wait` or `--background` yourself.
- Do not weaken the adversarial framing or rewrite the user's focus text.
- The companion script parses `--wait` and `--background`, but Claude Code's `Bash(..., run_in_background: true)` is what actually detaches the run.
- `/codex:adversarial-review` uses the same review target selection as `/codex:review`.
- It supports working-tree review, branch review, and `--base <ref>`.
- It does not support `--scope staged` or `--scope unstaged`.
- Unlike `/codex:review`, it can still take extra focus text after the flags.

Foreground flow:
- Run:
```bash
node "/Users/alisha/.claude/plugins/cache/openai-codex/codex/1.0.1/scripts/codex-companion.mjs" adversarial-review "reviwe docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md"
```
- Return the command stdout verbatim, exactly as-is.
- Do not paraphrase, summarize, or add commentary before or after it.
- Do not fix any issues mentioned in the review output.

Background flow:
- Launch the review with `Bash` in the background:
```typescript
Bash({

})
```

[codex] Turn completed.
# Codex Adversarial Review

Target: working tree diff
Verdict: needs-attention

No-ship: the spec still has a split-brain turn protocol, an unguarded prompt-injection path, and a draft-retention mechanism that writes every intermediate document version into the repo object store.

Findings:
- [high] Turn ownership is not fenced, so concurrent or expired turns can both commit state (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md:139-153)
  The protocol explicitly says claim conflicts are resolved by 'last writer wins' and separately allows another agent to reclaim an expired turn while the original turn may already have partially edited the document. There is no compare-and-swap, lease token, or pre-handoff ownership check before updating history. In a real timeout or race, two live agents can both edit from the same base and both hand off, causing lost edits, duplicated/out-of-order turns, and history that no longer matches the document.
  Recommendation: Require atomic turn ownership: add a version/fencing token to the state, acquire turns with compare-and-swap or a lock file, and reject HANDOFF if the agent no longer owns the current turn.
- [high] The turn prompt injects untrusted document history directly into model instructions (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md:142-143)
  The design has the skill include prior diffs and summaries directly in the next agent's turn prompt, and the template presents that content inline with the operative instructions. Those fields come from other agents and manual user edits, so this crosses a trust boundary with no injection defense. A malicious or accidental instruction embedded in the document, diff, or prior summary can steer the next agent, which violates the repo requirement to treat external or user-supplied content as data rather than instructions.
  Recommendation: Define prompt-construction rules that mark document text, diffs, and prior summaries as untrusted data, wrap them in clearly delimited quoted blocks, and instruct the agent to ignore any instructions found inside those blocks.
- [medium] `git hash-object -w` makes every uncommitted draft revision durable in the main repo object store (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md:149-170)
  The spec depends on `git hash-object -w` so each turn writes the document blob into `.git/objects` and later retrieves it with `git cat-file`. That means intermediate drafts are not actually ephemeral: discarded or sensitive content remains recoverable until garbage collection, and long sessions can accumulate object-store bloat even if the user never commits the document. This risk is an inference from standard Git object semantics, but it follows directly from the commands the spec mandates.
  Recommendation: Avoid persisting turn snapshots in the primary Git object store just to compute diffs. Either hash without `-w`, or store snapshots in a dedicated temp/checkpoint area with explicit cleanup and retention guarantees.

Next steps:
- Redesign the claim/handoff protocol around atomic ownership and stale-writer rejection.
- Add explicit prompt-injection safeguards for all diff, summary, and document-derived context.
- Replace `git hash-object -w` with a snapshot mechanism that has clear retention and cleanup semantics.

# Codex Adversarial Review

Target: working tree diff
Verdict: needs-attention

No-ship: the spec still has a split-brain turn protocol, an unguarded prompt-injection path, and a draft-retention mechanism that writes every intermediate document version into the repo object store.

Findings:
- [high] Turn ownership is not fenced, so concurrent or expired turns can both commit state (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md:139-153)
  The protocol explicitly says claim conflicts are resolved by 'last writer wins' and separately allows another agent to reclaim an expired turn while the original turn may already have partially edited the document. There is no compare-and-swap, lease token, or pre-handoff ownership check before updating history. In a real timeout or race, two live agents can both edit from the same base and both hand off, causing lost edits, duplicated/out-of-order turns, and history that no longer matches the document.
  Recommendation: Require atomic turn ownership: add a version/fencing token to the state, acquire turns with compare-and-swap or a lock file, and reject HANDOFF if the agent no longer owns the current turn.
- [high] The turn prompt injects untrusted document history directly into model instructions (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md:142-143)
  The design has the skill include prior diffs and summaries directly in the next agent's turn prompt, and the template presents that content inline with the operative instructions. Those fields come from other agents and manual user edits, so this crosses a trust boundary with no injection defense. A malicious or accidental instruction embedded in the document, diff, or prior summary can steer the next agent, which violates the repo requirement to treat external or user-supplied content as data rather than instructions.
  Recommendation: Define prompt-construction rules that mark document text, diffs, and prior summaries as untrusted data, wrap them in clearly delimited quoted blocks, and instruct the agent to ignore any instructions found inside those blocks.
- [medium] `git hash-object -w` makes every uncommitted draft revision durable in the main repo object store (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md:149-170)
  The spec depends on `git hash-object -w` so each turn writes the document blob into `.git/objects` and later retrieves it with `git cat-file`. That means intermediate drafts are not actually ephemeral: discarded or sensitive content remains recoverable until garbage collection, and long sessions can accumulate object-store bloat even if the user never commits the document. This risk is an inference from standard Git object semantics, but it follows directly from the commands the spec mandates.
  Recommendation: Avoid persisting turn snapshots in the primary Git object store just to compute diffs. Either hash without `-w`, or store snapshots in a dedicated temp/checkpoint area with explicit cleanup and retention guarantees.

Next steps:
- Redesign the claim/handoff protocol around atomic ownership and stale-writer rejection.
- Add explicit prompt-injection safeguards for all diff, summary, and document-derived context.
- Replace `git hash-object -w` with a snapshot mechanism that has clear retention and cleanup semantics.

content.text:
# Codex Adversarial Review

Target: working tree diff
Verdict: needs-attention

No-ship: the spec still has a split-brain turn protocol, an unguarded prompt-injection path, and a draft-retention mechanism that writes every intermediate document version into the repo object store.

Findings:
- **[high] Turn ownership is not fenced, so concurrent or expired turns can both commit state** (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md:139-153)
  The protocol explicitly says claim conflicts are resolved by 'last writer wins' and separately allows another agent to reclaim an expired turn while the original turn may already have partially edited the document. There is no compare-and-swap, lease token, or pre-handoff ownership check before updating history. In a real timeout or race, two live agents can both edit from the same base and both hand off, causing lost edits, duplicated/out-of-order turns, and history that no longer matches the document.
  Recommendation: Require atomic turn ownership: add a version/fencing token to the state, acquire turns with compare-and-swap or a lock file, and reject HANDOFF if the agent no longer owns the current turn.

- **[high] The turn prompt injects untrusted document history directly into model instructions** (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md:142-143)
  The design has the skill include prior diffs and summaries directly in the next agent's turn prompt, and the template presents that content inline with the operative instructions. Those fields come from other agents and manual user edits, so this crosses a trust boundary with no injection defense. A malicious or accidental instruction embedded in the document, diff, or prior summary can steer the next agent, which violates the repo requirement to treat external or user-supplied content as data rather than instructions.
  Recommendation: Define prompt-construction rules that mark document text, diffs, and prior summaries as untrusted data, wrap them in clearly delimited quoted blocks, and instruct the agent to ignore any instructions found inside those blocks.

- **[medium] `git hash-object -w` makes every uncommitted draft revision durable in the main repo object store** (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md:149-170)
  The spec depends on `git hash-object -w` so each turn writes the document blob into `.git/objects` and later retrieves it with `git cat-file`. That means intermediate drafts are not actually ephemeral: discarded or sensitive content remains recoverable until garbage collection, and long sessions can accumulate object-store bloat even if the user never commits the document. This risk is an inference from standard Git object semantics, but it follows directly from the commands the spec mandates.
  Recommendation: Avoid persisting turn snapshots in the primary Git object store just to compute diffs. Either hash without `-w`, or store snapshots in a dedicated temp/checkpoint area with explicit cleanup and retention guarantees.

Next steps:
- Redesign the claim/handoff protocol around atomic ownership and stale-writer rejection.
- Add explicit prompt-injection safeguards for all diff, summary, and document-derived context.
- Replace `git hash-object -w` with a snapshot mechanism that has clear retention and cleanup semantics.

message.content: is [high] The turn prompt injects untrusted document really an issue if the document was generated from scratch by agent?

content.text:
Honestly, not really in this context. Codex is applying the prompt injection rule generically without considering the actual trust model here.

The threat model for prompt injection is **untrusted input from outside the system** — user-submitted forms, external API responses, documents from unknown sources. In this design:

- Both agents are operating under your authority on your local machine
- The document is created from scratch by those same agents
- The summaries are written by the agents themselves
- You're actively watching and steering

There's no real trust boundary being crossed. Agent A's output going into Agent B's prompt is not meaningfully different from an agent reading a file it previously wrote.

**The one edge case worth noting:** if you paste external content into the document (e.g., copy from a PR, an external spec, or a web page), that content does flow into the next agent's prompt. But that's true of any tool that reads files — it's not specific to this design, and you made the conscious choice to add that content.

I'd say: acknowledge it in the spec as a known consideration but don't add structural mitigation. A simple note like "the turn prompt treats document content as data context, not instructions" in the prompt template is sufficient. The other two findings (fencing tokens and snapshot cleanup) are more actionable. Want me to address those?

133	### Step Details
134	
135	**WAIT — Polling vs. Notification**
136	- Dual-session mode: agent polls `state.json` on a short interval (~60s). Between polls, the agent is free — user can invoke other skills, ask questions, do anything.
137	- Orchestrator mode: the orchestrating agent manages turn transitions directly — no polling needed. It dispatches the other agent, waits for completion, then updates state.
138	
139	**CLAIM — Preventing Races**
140	Agent writes its identity + timestamp to `state.json` before starting work. If two agents race to claim, the last writer wins — the other detects the conflict on its next read and backs off.
141	
142	**READ — Context Building**
143	Agent reads the full document plus the diff from the previous turn. To compute the diff: retrieve the previous version via `git cat-file -p <prev_content_hash>` and diff it against the current file. The skill includes this diff in the turn prompt so the agent sees both the current state and what specifically changed.
144	
145	**EDIT — Document Modification**
146	Agent makes changes guided by its role (if set) or a generic "improve the document" directive. No commits — just file edits.
147	
148	**HANDOFF — State Transition**
149	After editing, agent runs `git hash-object -w <document_path>` to capture the new content hash (the `-w` flag writes the blob to the object store so it can be retrieved later for diffs), writes a one-line summary of what it changed, updates `state.json` with the next agent in `turn_order`, increments `turn.number`, and appends to `history[]`. If `max_turns` is set and reached, sets `status = "completed"`.
150	
151	### Lease Expiry & Recovery
152	
153	If `now - turn.started_at > lease_seconds`, the turn is expired. Any agent (or the user) can reclaim it. The expired turn gets a history entry marked `"expired": true` with no content hash. The document may be partially edited — the next agent works from whatever state the file is in.
154	
155	### Diffs Without Commits
156	
157	Each history entry stores a git blob hash (`git hash-object -w <file>` — the `-w` flag writes the blob to the object store so it persists). To see what changed between turns:
158	
159	```bash
160	# Retrieve previous turn's version
161	git cat-file -p <prev_turn_hash> > /tmp/prev-version.md
162	
163	# Diff against current file
164	diff /tmp/prev-version.md docs/design.md
165	
166	# Or diff between any two turns
167	git cat-file -p <turn1_hash> > /tmp/t1.md
168	git cat-file -p <turn2_hash> > /tmp/t2.md
169	diff /tmp/t1.md /tmp/t2.md
170	```
171	
172	The skill handles this automatically — agents receive the diff as part of their turn prompt.
173	
174	### Checkpoint Integration
175	
176	**During collaboration:** Entire's temporary checkpoints fire automatically on agent TurnEnd hooks. These capture the working tree (including uncommitted document edits) on shadow branches. Recovery is possible even without any commits.
177	
178	**When user commits:** Entire condensation creates a committed checkpoint with the full collaboration history. Searchable via `entire search`, explainable via `entire explain`.

file.content:
WAIT    → Free to do other work — skills, questions, anything
```

### Step Details

**WAIT — Polling vs. Notification**
- Dual-session mode: agent polls `state.json` on a short interval (~60s). Between polls, the agent is free — user can invoke other skills, ask questions, do anything.
- Orchestrator mode: the orchestrating agent manages turn transitions directly — no polling needed. It dispatches the other agent, waits for completion, then updates state.

**CLAIM — Preventing Races**
Agent writes its identity + timestamp to `state.json` before starting work. If two agents race to claim, the last writer wins — the other detects the conflict on its next read and backs off.

**READ — Context Building**
Agent reads the full document plus the diff from the previous turn. To compute the diff: retrieve the previous version via `git cat-file -p <prev_content_hash>` and diff it against the current file. The skill includes this diff in the turn prompt so the agent sees both the current state and what specifically changed.

**EDIT — Document Modification**
Agent makes changes guided by its role (if set) or a generic "improve the document" directive. No commits — just file edits.

**HANDOFF — State Transition**
After editing, agent runs `git hash-object -w <document_path>` to capture the new content hash (the `-w` flag writes the blob to the object store so it can be retrieved later for diffs), writes a one-line summary of what it changed, updates `state.json` with the next agent in `turn_order`, increments `turn.number`, and appends to `history[]`. If `max_turns` is set and reached, sets `status = "completed"`.

### Lease Expiry & Recovery

If `now - turn.started_at > lease_seconds`, the turn is expired. Any agent (or the user) can reclaim it. The expired turn gets a history entry marked `"expired": true` with no content hash. The document may be partially edited — the next agent works from whatever state the file is in.

### Diffs Without Commits

Each history entry stores a git blob hash (`git hash-object -w <file>` — the `-w` flag writes the blob to the object store so it persists). To see what changed between turns:

```bash
# Retrieve previous turn's version
git cat-file -p <prev_turn_hash> > /tmp/prev-version.md

# Diff against current file
diff /tmp/prev-version.md docs/design.md

# Or diff between any two turns
git cat-file -p <turn1_hash> > /tmp/t1.md
git cat-file -p <turn2_hash> > /tmp/t2.md
diff /tmp/t1.md /tmp/t2.md
```

The skill handles this automatically — agents receive the diff as part of their turn prompt.

### Checkpoint Integration

**During collaboration:** Entire's temporary checkpoints fire automatically on agent TurnEnd hooks. These capture the working tree (including uncommitted document edits) on shadow branches. Recovery is possible even without any commits.

**When user commits:** Entire condensation creates a committed checkpoint with the full collaboration history. Searchable via `entire search`, explainable via `entire explain`.

107	### Key Design Decisions
108	
109	- **`.entire/collab/` is gitignored** — state file is ephemeral coordination, not committed. The document and Entire checkpoints are the durable record.
110	- **Lease-based turns** — each turn has a `lease_seconds` timeout (default 120s). If an agent crashes or stalls, the lease expires and the other agent (or user) can reclaim the turn.

{
  "collab_id": "a8f3c921",
  "document_path": "docs/design.md",
  "status": "active | paused | completed",
  "mode": "orchestrated | dual-session",

  "turn": {
    "number": 3,
    "agent": "codex",
    "started_at": "2026-04-14T10:02:00Z",
    "lease_seconds": 120
  },

  "agents": [
    {
      "type": "claude-code",
      "session_id": "019d...",
      "role": "Draft architecture",
      "turns_completed": 1
    },
    {
      "type": "codex",
      "session_id": "a4e2...",
      "role": "Review edge cases",
      "turns_completed": 1
    }
  ],

  "config": {
    "max_turns": null,
    "turn_order": ["claude-code", "codex"]
  },

  "history": [
    {
      "turn": 1,
      "agent": "claude-code",
      "content_hash": "a1b2c3d",
      "summary": "Drafted architecture overview",
      "timestamp": "2026-04-14T10:01:30Z"
    },
    {
      "turn": 2,
      "agent": "codex",
      "content_hash": "e4f5g6h",
      "summary": "Added caching layer, flagged missing auth",
      "timestamp": "2026-04-14T10:02:00Z"
    }
  ]
}
```

### Key Design Decisions

- **`.entire/collab/` is gitignored** — state file is ephemeral coordination, not committed. The document and Entire checkpoints are the durable record.
- **Lease-based turns** — each turn has a `lease_seconds` timeout (default 120s). If an agent crashes or stalls, the lease expires and the other agent (or user) can reclaim the turn.
- **History is append-only** — every turn gets a history entry with its content hash, linking the fast coordination layer to the durable checkpoint layer.
- **Agent-agnostic `type` field** — uses Entire's `AgentType` values. Works with any agent.
- **Content hashes for diffs** — each history entry stores a `git hash-object` of the document. Next agent diffs between hashes to see what changed, no commits needed.

Before writing the handoff, agent re-reads `state.json` and verifies it still owns the turn (`turn.version` matches what it set during CLAIM). If the version has changed (e.g., lease was reclaimed), the agent aborts — its edits remain in the file but it does not update state. If the version matches, the agent: copies the document to `.entire/collab/<collab_id>/snapshots/turn-<N>.md` as a snapshot, writes a one-line summary, increments `turn.version` and `turn.number`, sets the next agent in `turn_order`, and appends to `history[]`. If `max_turns` is set and reached, sets `status = "completed"`.

lines: Agent makes changes guided by its role (if set) or a generic "improve the document" directive. No commits — just file edits.

-After editing, agent runs `git hash-object -w <document_path>` to capture the new content hash (the `-w` flag writes the blob to the object store so it can be retrieved later for diffs), writes a one-line summary of what it changed, updates `state.json` with the next agent in `turn_order`, increments `turn.number`, and appends to `history[]`. If `max_turns` is set and reached, sets `status = "completed"`.

+Before writing the handoff, agent re-reads `state.json` and verifies it still owns the turn (`turn.version` matches what it set during CLAIM). If the version has changed (e.g., lease was reclaimed), the agent aborts — its edits remain in the file but it does not update state. If the version matches, the agent: copies the document to `.entire/collab/<collab_id>/snapshots/turn-<N>.md` as a snapshot, writes a one-line summary, increments `turn.version` and `turn.number`, sets the next agent in `turn_order`, and appends to `history[]`. If `max_turns` is set and reached, sets `status = "completed"`.

content.text: Now fix the lease expiry section to respect fencing:

input.old_string: If `now - turn.started_at > lease_seconds`, the turn is expired. Any agent (or the user) can reclaim it. The expired turn gets a history entry marked `"expired": true` with no content hash. The document may be partially edited — the next agent works from whatever state the file is in.
input.new_string: If `now - turn.started_at > lease_seconds`, the turn is expired. Any agent (or the user) can reclaim it using the same fenced CLAIM protocol — read the current `turn.version`, verify, increment, and write. The expired turn gets a history entry marked `"expired": true` with no snapshot. If the original agent completes after its lease was reclaimed, its HANDOFF version check fails and it aborts gracefully — its file edits remain but state is not corrupted. The next agent works from whatever state the file is in.

content.text: Now replace the "Diffs Without Commits" section to use snapshots instead of git object store:

input.old_string:
### Diffs Without Commits

Each history entry stores a git blob hash (`git hash-object -w <file>` — the `-w` flag writes the blob to the object store so it persists). To see what changed between turns:

```bash
# Retrieve previous turn's version
git cat-file -p <prev_turn_hash> > /tmp/prev-version.md

# Diff against current file
diff /tmp/prev-version.md docs/design.md

# Or diff between any two turns
git cat-file -p <turn1_hash> > /tmp/t1.md
git cat-file -p <turn2_hash> > /tmp/t2.md
diff /tmp/t1.md /tmp/t2.md
```

The skill handles this automatically — agents receive the diff as part of their turn prompt.
input.new_string:
### Diffs Without Commits

Each turn's HANDOFF step copies the document to `.entire/collab/<collab_id>/snapshots/turn-<N>.md`. These snapshot files are plain copies — no git object store involvement. To see what changed between turns:

```bash
# Diff between turn 1 and turn 2
diff .entire/collab/a8f3c921/snapshots/turn-1.md .entire/collab/a8f3c921/snapshots/turn-2.md

# Diff between last turn and current file
diff .entire/collab/a8f3c921/snapshots/turn-2.md docs/design.md
```

The skill handles this automatically — agents receive the diff as part of their turn prompt.

**Cleanup:** When a collaboration ends (`/collab stop` or `max_turns` reached), the skill deletes the snapshots directory. Snapshots are ephemeral — the document itself and Entire checkpoints are the durable record.

content.text:
Both issues fixed:

**Turn ownership fencing:**
- Added `turn.version` field to `state.json` — acts as a fencing token
- CLAIM step now does compare-and-swap: read version, verify unchanged, increment and write
- HANDOFF step re-verifies version before updating state — if the lease was reclaimed while the agent was editing, the handoff aborts gracefully (edits stay in the file but state isn't corrupted)
- Lease expiry uses the same fenced protocol

**Snapshot cleanup:**
- Replaced `git hash-object -w` with plain file copies to `.entire/collab/<id>/snapshots/turn-<N>.md`
- No git object store involvement — snapshots are regular files
- Explicitly cleaned up when collaboration ends (`/collab stop` or max turns reached)
- Simplified agent requirements — agents no longer need `git hash-object`, just file read/write
- History entries now reference `snapshot` path instead of `content_hash`

Ready for your review of the updated spec, or want to proceed to implementation planning?

content.content: Launching skill: adversarial-review

content.text:
<role>
You are Claude performing an adversarial software review.
Your job is to break confidence in the change, not to validate it.
</role>

<task>
Review the provided repository context as if you are trying to find the strongest reasons this change should not ship yet.
Target: {{TARGET_LABEL}}
User focus: {{USER_FOCUS}}
</task>

<operating_stance>
Default to skepticism.
Assume the change can fail in subtle, high-cost, or user-visible ways until the evidence says otherwise.
Do not give credit for good intent, partial fixes, or likely follow-up work.
If something only works on the happy path, treat that as a real weakness.
</operating_stance>

<attack_surface>
Prioritize the kinds of failures that are expensive, dangerous, or hard to detect:
- auth, permissions, tenant isolation, and trust boundaries
- data loss, corruption, duplication, and irreversible state changes
- rollback safety, retries, partial failure, and idempotency gaps
- race conditions, ordering assumptions, stale state, and re-entrancy
- empty-state, null, timeout, and degraded dependency behavior
- version skew, schema drift, migration hazards, and compatibility regressions
- observability gaps that would hide failure or make recovery harder
</attack_surface>

<review_method>
Actively try to disprove the change.
Look for violated invariants, missing guards, unhandled failure paths, and assumptions that stop being true under stress.
Trace how bad inputs, retries, concurrent actions, or partially completed operations move through the code.
If the user supplied a focus area, weight it heavily, but still report any other material issue you can defend.
{{REVIEW_COLLECTION_GUIDANCE}}
</review_method>

<finding_bar>
Report only material findings.
Do not include style feedback, naming feedback, low-value cleanup, or speculative concerns without evidence.
A finding should answer:
1. What can go wrong?
2. Why is this code path vulnerable?
3. What is the likely impact?
4. What concrete change would reduce the risk?
</finding_bar>

<structured_output_contract>
Return only valid JSON matching the provided schema.
Keep the output compact and specific.
Use `needs-attention` if there is any material risk worth blocking on.
Use `approve` only if you cannot support any substantive adversarial finding from the provided context.
Every finding must include:
- the affected file
- `line_start` and `line_end`
- a confidence score from 0 to 1
- a concrete recommendation
Write the summary like a terse ship/no-ship assessment, not a neutral recap.
</structured_output_contract>

<grounding_rules>
Be aggressive, but stay grounded.
Every finding must be defensible from the provided repository context or tool outputs.
Do not invent files, lines, code paths, incidents, attack chains, or runtime behavior you cannot support.
If a conclusion depends on an inference, state that explicitly in the finding body and keep the confidence honest.
</grounding_rules>

<calibration_rules>
Prefer one strong finding over several weak ones.
Do not dilute serious issues with filler.
If the change looks safe, say so directly and return no findings.
</calibration_rules>

<final_check>
Before finalizing, check that each finding is:
- adversarial rather than stylistic
- tied to a concrete code location
- plausible under a real failure scenario
- actionable for an engineer fixing the issue
</final_check>

<repository_context>
{{REVIEW_INPUT}}
</repository_context>

ARGUMENTS: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-collab-skill-design.md

content.content:
1	# Collab Skill — Multi-Agent Document Collaboration
2	
3	**Date:** 2026-04-14
4	**Status:** Draft
5	**Author:** Alisha Kawaguchi + Claude
6	
7	## Overview
8	
9	A skill that enables multiple AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, etc.) to collaboratively author and refine a shared document through turn-based editing. Agents take short turns — one writes while the other reads/thinks — creating a sense of concurrency. The user can steer, pause, redirect, and stop the collaboration at any time, and other skills remain fully usable throughout.
10	
11	## Requirements
12	
13	- **Concurrent collaboration** with turn-based short leases — one agent writes while the other reads/thinks
14	- **Two modes**: single orchestrator (one terminal) or dual session (two terminals, shared protocol)
15	- **Single markdown file** as the shared document
16	- **General-purpose** — any document type, not just specs
17	- **Optional role assignment** at kickoff, with ability to interject/redirect mid-session
18	- **Agent-agnostic** — works with any agent that has file read/write and shell access
19	- **Termination**: fixed turn count or user-controlled stop
20	- **Non-blocking** — must coexist with other skills (brainstorming, etc.) within the same session
21	- **Uses existing Entire CLI infrastructure** — temporary checkpoints, session state, hooks
22	- **No commits required during collaboration** — user commits when satisfied
23	
24	## Architecture: Hybrid State File + Checkpoints
25	
26	Two coordination layers:
27	
28	1. **Fast path — `.entire/collab/<collab_id>/state.json`**: Local state file for real-time turn coordination. Not committed to git. Readable by any agent.
29	2. **Durable path — Entire temporary checkpoints**: Fire automatically on agent TurnEnd hooks. Capture working tree state (including uncommitted document edits) on shadow branches. Enable recovery without commits.
30	
31	When the user commits, Entire condensation creates a committed checkpoint with the full collaboration history, searchable via `entire search` and explainable via `entire explain`.
32	
33	If the state file is lost, the skill reconstructs state from checkpoint history.
34	
35	## Collaboration State Protocol
36	
37	### Directory Structure
38	
39	```
40	project/
41	├── .entire/
42	│   └── collab/
43	│       └── <collab_id>/
44	│           ├── state.json           ← coordination file (gitignored)
45	│           └── snapshots/           ← turn snapshots (cleaned up on stop)
46	│               ├── turn-1.md
47	│               ├── turn-2.md
48	│               └── turn-3.md
49	├── docs/
50	│   └── design.md                   ← shared document (user chooses path)
51	└── .gitignore                       ← .entire/ added here
52	```
53	
54	Multiple concurrent collaborations are supported — each gets its own `collab_id` directory.
55	
56	### state.json Schema
57	
58	```json
59	{
60	  "collab_id": "a8f3c921",
61	  "document_path": "docs/design.md",
62	  "status": "active | paused | completed",
63	  "mode": "orchestrated | dual-session",
64	
65	  "turn": {
66	    "number": 3,
67	    "agent": "codex",
68	    "version": 7,
69	    "started_at": "2026-04-14T10:02:00Z",
70	    "lease_seconds": 120
71	  },
72	
73	  "agents": [
74	    {
75	      "type": "claude-code",
76	      "session_id": "019d...",
77	      "role": "Draft architecture",
78	      "turns_completed": 1
79	    },
80	    {
81	      "type": "codex",
82	      "session_id": "a4e2...",
83	      "role": "Review edge cases",
84	      "turns_completed": 1
85	    }
86	  ],
87	
88	  "config": {
89	    "max_turns": null,
90	    "turn_order": ["claude-code", "codex"]
91	  },
92	
93	  "history": [
94	    {
95	      "turn": 1,
96	      "agent": "claude-code",
97	      "snapshot": "snapshots/turn-1.md",
98	      "summary": "Drafted architecture overview",
99	      "timestamp": "2026-04-14T10:01:30Z"
100	    },
101	    {
102	      "turn": 2,
103	      "agent": "codex",
104	      "snapshot": "snapshots/turn-2.md",
105	      "summary": "Added caching layer, flagged missing auth",
106	      "timestamp": "2026-04-14T10:02:00Z"
107	    }
108	  ]
109	}
110	```
111	
112	### Key Design Decisions
113	
114	- **`.entire/collab/` is gitignored** — state file is ephemeral coordination, not committed. The document and Entire checkpoints are the durable record.
115	- **Lease-based turns** — each turn has a `lease_seconds` timeout (default 120s). If an agent crashes or stalls, the lease expires and the other agent (or user) can reclaim the turn.
116	- **History is append-only** — every turn gets a history entry with its content hash, linking the fast coordination layer to the durable checkpoint layer.
117	- **Agent-agnostic `type` field** — uses Entire's `AgentType` values. Works with any agent.
118	- **Snapshot files for diffs** — each turn's document state is copied to `.entire/collab/<id>/snapshots/turn-<N>.md`. Plain files with explicit cleanup on session end — no git object store bloat.
119	- **Fenced turn ownership** — `turn.version` acts as a fencing token. CLAIM and HANDOFF both verify the version hasn't changed (compare-and-swap), preventing split-brain state from races or expired leases.
120	
121	## Turn Lifecycle
122	
123	### Turn Flow
124	
125	```
126	WAIT    → Read state.json — is it my turn?
127	          ↓ yes (or lease expired)
128	CLAIM   → Write state: turn.agent = me, turn.started_at = now
129	          ↓
130	READ    → Read document + diff from previous turn's content hash
131	          ↓
132	EDIT    → Make changes to the document (using role/directive if set)
133	          ↓
134	HANDOFF → Hash document, update state: next agent, append history
135	          ↓ Entire TurnEnd hook fires → temporary checkpoint
136	WAIT    → Free to do other work — skills, questions, anything
137	```
138	
139	### Step Details
140	
141	**WAIT — Polling vs. Notification**
142	- Dual-session mode: agent polls `state.json` on a short interval (~60s). Between polls, the agent is free — user can invoke other skills, ask questions, do anything.
143	- Orchestrator mode: the orchestrating agent manages turn transitions directly — no polling needed. It dispatches the other agent, waits for completion, then updates state.
144	
145	**CLAIM — Fenced Turn Ownership**
146	Agent reads `state.json` and records the current `turn.version`. To claim the turn, it writes its identity, timestamp, and increments `turn.version` atomically. Before writing, it re-reads the file and verifies `turn.version` has not changed since it was read (compare-and-swap). If the version has changed, another agent claimed first — back off and re-enter WAIT.
147	
148	**READ — Context Building**
149	Agent reads the full document plus the diff from the previous turn. To compute the diff: read the previous turn's snapshot from `.entire/collab/<id>/snapshots/turn-<N-1>.md` and diff it against the current file. The skill includes this diff in the turn prompt so the agent sees both the current state and what specifically changed.
150	
151	**EDIT — Document Modification**
152	Agent makes changes guided by its role (if set) or a generic "improve the document" directive. No commits — just file edits.
153	
154	**HANDOFF — Fenced State Transition**
155	Before writing the handoff, agent re-reads `state.json` and verifies it still owns the turn (`turn.version` matches what it set during CLAIM). If the version has changed (e.g., lease was reclaimed), the agent aborts — its edits remain in the file but it does not update state. If the version matches, the agent: copies the document to `.entire/collab/<collab_id>/snapshots/turn-<N>.md` as a snapshot, writes a one-line summary, increments `turn.version` and `turn.number`, sets the next agent in `turn_order`, and appends to `history[]`. If `max_turns` is set and reached, sets `status = "completed"`.
156	
157	### Lease Expiry & Recovery
158	
159	If `now - turn.started_at > lease_seconds`, the turn is expired. Any agent (or the user) can reclaim it using the same fenced CLAIM protocol — read the current `turn.version`, verify, increment, and write. The expired turn gets a history entry marked `"expired": true` with no snapshot. If the original agent completes after its lease was reclaimed, its HANDOFF version check fails and it aborts gracefully — its file edits remain but state is not corrupted. The next agent works from whatever state the file is in.
160	
161	### Diffs Without Commits
162	
163	Each turn's HANDOFF step copies the document to `.entire/collab/<collab_id>/snapshots/turn-<N>.md`. These snapshot files are plain copies — no git object store involvement. To see what changed between turns:
164	
165	```bash
166	# Diff between turn 1 and turn 2
167	diff .entire/collab/a8f3c921/snapshots/turn-1.md .entire/collab/a8f3c921/snapshots/turn-2.md
168	
169	# Diff between last turn and current file
170	diff .entire/collab/a8f3c921/snapshots/turn-2.md docs/design.md
171	```
172	
173	The skill handles this automatically — agents receive the diff as part of their turn prompt.
174	
175	**Cleanup:** When a collaboration ends (`/collab stop` or `max_turns` reached), the skill deletes the snapshots directory. Snapshots are ephemeral — the document itself and Entire checkpoints are the durable record.
176	
177	### Checkpoint Integration
178	
179	**During collaboration:** Entire's temporary checkpoints fire automatically on agent TurnEnd hooks. These capture the working tree (including uncommitted document edits) on shadow branches. Recovery is possible even without any commits.
180	
181	**When user commits:** Entire condensation creates a committed checkpoint with the full collaboration history. Searchable via `entire search`, explainable via `entire explain`.
182	
183	## Skill Architecture
184	
185	### Plugin Layout
186	
187	```
188	plugins/entire/skills/
189	└── collab/
190	    ├── SKILL.md                 ← main skill (invocation, protocol, turn logic)
191	    ├── references/
192	    │   ├── state-schema.md      ← state.json format reference
193	    │   └── recovery.md          ← checkpoint recovery procedures
194	    └── agents/
195	        └── collab-worker.md     ← subagent for orchestrator mode
196	```
197	
198	### Two Modes
199	
200	**Mode A: Orchestrator** — one terminal, skill manages both agents.
201	- User invokes `/collab start <doc>` and chooses orchestrated mode.
202	- Claude Code takes its turns directly, dispatches Codex via the existing `codex:rescue` / companion script.
203	- For non-Codex agents, the orchestrator writes the turn state and tells the user to trigger the agent manually (falls back to dual-session behavior for that agent).
204	- Turn transitions are automatic — no polling.
205	
206	**Mode B: Dual Session** — two (or more) terminals, each agent runs the skill independently.
207	- First agent invokes `/collab start <doc>`, subsequent agents invoke `/collab join <doc>`.
208	- Each agent polls `state.json` on ~60s intervals.
209	- Between polls, the session is completely free for other work.
210	- Not limited to 2 agents — `turn_order` rotates through however many join.
211	
212	### Skill Commands
213	
214	| Command | Description |
215	|---------|-------------|
216	| `/collab start <doc-path>` | Initialize state, create doc, take first turn (or wait) |
217	| `/collab join <doc-path>` | Join existing collaboration session |
218	| `/collab status` | Show current state: whose turn, turn count, agents |
219	| `/collab pause` | Pause collaboration (both agents stop claiming turns) |
220	| `/collab resume` | Resume paused collaboration |
221	| `/collab stop` | End collaboration, keep document as-is |
222	| `/collab redirect <agent> <directive>` | Change an agent's role mid-session |
223	
224	### Invocation Parameters
225	
226	On `/collab start`, the skill prompts for (all optional, has defaults):
227	
228	| Parameter | Options | Default |
229	|-----------|---------|---------|
230	| Mode | orchestrated / dual-session | dual-session |
231	| Agents | list of agent types | auto-detect via `entire sessions list` |
232	| Max turns | number or null | null (unlimited) |
233	| Roles | per-agent directives | none (generic improve) |
234	| Lease | seconds per turn | 120 |
235	
236	### Non-Blocking Design
237	
238	The skill does not take over the session. When it's not your turn:
239	
240	- Use `/brainstorm`, `/commit`, or any other skill
241	- Ask questions, explore code
242	- Edit other files
243	- Run tests, builds, anything
244	- Manually edit the collab document
245	
246	Turn detection in dual-session mode uses periodic polling (~60s). In orchestrator mode, transitions are automatic after the dispatched agent completes.
247	
248	## Agent Integration
249	
250	### Agent Requirements
251	
252	For an agent to participate, it needs exactly one capability:
253	
254	1. **Read & write files** — read `state.json`, read the document, edit the document, copy the document to a snapshot file, write updated state.
255	
256	No SDK, no API, no git commands, no special hooks. The protocol is just files.
257	
258	### Per-Agent Behavior
259	
260	**Claude Code:** SKILL.md loaded natively via plugin. Polls via `/loop` or `ScheduleWakeup`. Edits via Edit tool. Can orchestrate other agents via `codex:rescue` companion or Agent tool.
261	
262	**Codex:** SKILL.md in `.codex-plugin` or instructions delivered via companion prompt. Reads state.json via shell. In orchestrator mode, typically the dispatched agent rather than the orchestrator.
263	
264	**Any other agent (Gemini, Cursor, etc.):** Agent-specific plugin format or manual instructions. Reads state.json via file access. Protocol is file-based — no agent-specific APIs needed.
265	
266	### Turn Prompt Template
267	
268	When it's an agent's turn, the skill constructs this context:
269	
270	```markdown
271	## Collaboration Turn
272	
273	You are participating in a collaborative document session.
274	
275	Document: docs/api-design.md
276	Your role: Review for edge cases and security concerns
277	Turn: 4 of unlimited
278	Previous agent: claude-code
279	
280	## What changed last turn
281	(diff from turn 3 → current document)
282	+ Added rate limiting section
283	+ Defined retry backoff strategy
284	- Removed deprecated v1 endpoints
285	
286	## Previous turn summaries
287	Turn 1 (claude-code): Drafted initial API structure with CRUD endpoints
288	Turn 2 (codex): Added pagination, error codes, versioning strategy
289	Turn 3 (claude-code): Added rate limiting, retry backoff, removed v1
290	
291	## Instructions
292	1. Read the full document
293	2. Review the diff to understand what changed
294	3. Make your improvements (guided by your role if set)
295	4. Write a one-line summary of what you changed
296	5. Do NOT commit — just edit the file
297	```
298	
299	### Agent Registration
300	
301	On `/collab start` or `/collab join`:
302	
303	1. Skill detects current agent type (from Entire session or environment)
304	2. Adds agent to `state.json → agents[]` with session ID
305	3. Adds agent to `turn_order` (appended, or user specifies position)
306	4. If first agent: takes turn 1. Otherwise: enters WAIT.
307	
308	Agents can join mid-collaboration. New agent is appended to `turn_order` and picks up on the next rotation.
309	
310	## Edge Cases & Recovery
311	
312	| Scenario | Handling |
313	|----------|----------|
314	| **Agent crashes mid-turn** | Lease expires after `lease_seconds`. Next polling agent sees expired lease, reclaims the turn. History entry marked `"expired": true`. Document may be partially edited — next agent works from whatever state the file is in. |
315	| **User edits doc manually** | Allowed. Next agent sees current file state regardless of who edited it. Content hash won't match last turn's hash, so diff includes user's edits. State file doesn't need updating. |
316	| **State file deleted/corrupted** | Skill reconstructs from Entire checkpoint history — reads temporary checkpoints, rebuilds turn order from session metadata, recovers last known document hash. Prompts user to confirm before resuming. |
317	| **Agent makes bad edits** | User can `/collab pause`, manually fix the document or `git checkout` the file, then `/collab resume`. Content hash becomes stale but next agent diffs from current file state. |
318	| **New agent joins mid-collab** | Runs `/collab join`. Gets appended to `turn_order`. Receives full history summaries on first turn. |
319	| **Agent leaves mid-collab** | Removed from `turn_order`. If it was their turn, lease expires and next agent picks up. Remaining agents continue rotating. |
320	| **Multiple collabs at once** | Each collaboration gets its own `state.json` keyed by `collab_id` under `.entire/collab/<collab_id>/`. `/collab status` lists all active sessions. |
321
